reasoning: The question asks whether there will be a 10x increase in 'Violence against civilians' events in Ethiopia over a base value of ~45.667 (meaning >456 events in a single 30-day period). Historically, Ethiopia has never approached this level of recorded violence against civilians in a single month. During extreme periods of the Tigray War and the Amhara conflict, events of this specific classification reached roughly 50-150 per month, far short of the 457 threshold. ACLED methodology has remained stable, precluding a structural data jump, and recent updates from May 2025 show normal activity levels (no 10x surge). Since over 3/4s of the time for the first resolution date has already elapsed with no observed spike, and such an escalation would take unprecedented nationwide targeting and granular tracking, the probability is near absolute zero. I am bound by the system's minimum probability rule of 0.05, which I assign to the near-term dates, adding slight uncertainty for later in the year.
